# Skill: agent-browser # Browser Automation with agent-browser ## Prerequisites (Required for each new session) Before starting any new browser automation tasks/work/session, you MUST: 1. **Start the CDP browser** by running: ```bash node .opencode/skills/agent-browser/templates/launch-chrome-standalone.cjs ``` 2. **Connect to the browser** on port 9222: ```bash agent-browser connect 9222 ``` 3. Then proceed with your browser tasks. > Note: This start and connect sequence only needs to be done once at the beginning of a fresh new session. ## Quick start ```bash agent-browser open # Navigate to page agent-browser snapshot -i # Get interactive elements with refs agent-browser click @e1 # Click element by ref agent-browser fill @e2 "text" # Fill input by ref agent-browser close # Close browser ``` ## Core workflow 1. Navigate: `agent-browser open ` 2. Snapshot: `agent-browser snapshot -i` (returns elements with refs like `@e1`, `@e2`) 3. Interact using refs from the snapshot 4. Re-snapshot after navigation or significant DOM changes ## Commands ### Navigation ```bash agent-browser open # Navigate to URL (aliases: goto, navigate) # Supports: https://, http://, file://, about:, data:// # Auto-prepends https:// if no protocol given agent-browser back # Go back agent-browser forward # Go forward agent-browser reload # Reload page agent-browser close # Close browser (aliases: quit, exit) agent-browser connect 9222 # Connect to browser via CDP port ``` ### Snapshot (page analysis) ```bash agent-browser snapshot # Full accessibility tree agent-browser snapshot -i # Interactive elements only (recommended) agent-browser snapshot -c # Compact output agent-browser snapshot -d 3 # Limit depth to 3 agent-browser snapshot -s "#main" # Scope to CSS selector ``` ### Interactions (use @refs from snapshot) ```bash agent-browser click @e1 # Click agent-browser dblclick @e1 # Double-click agent-browser focus @e1 # Focus element agent-browser fill @e2 "text" # Clear and type agent-browser type @e2 "text" # Type without clearing agent-browser press Enter # Press key (alias: key) agent-browser press Control+a # Key combination agent-browser keydown Shift # Hold key down agent-browser keyup Shift # Release key agent-browser hover @e1 # Hover agent-browser check @e1 # Check checkbox agent-browser uncheck @e1 # Uncheck checkbox agent-browser select @e1 "value" # Select dropdown option agent-browser select @e1 "a" "b" # Select multiple options agent-browser scroll down 500 # Scroll page (default: down 300px) agent-browser scrollintoview @e1 # Scroll element into view (alias: scrollinto) agent-browser drag @e1 @e2 # Drag and drop agent-browser upload @e1 file.pdf # Upload files ``` ### Get information ```bash agent-browser get text @e1 # Get element text agent-browser get html @e1 # Get innerHTML agent-browser get value @e1 # Get input value agent-browser get attr @e1 href # Get attribute agent-browser get title # Get page title agent-browser get url # Get current URL agent-browser get count ".item" # Count matching elements agent-browser get box @e1 # Get bounding box agent-browser get styles @e1 # Get computed styles (font, color, bg, etc.) ``` ### Check state ```bash agent-browser is visible @e1 # Check if visible agent-browser is enabled @e1 # Check if enabled agent-browser is checked @e1 # Check if checked ``` ### Screenshots & PDF ```bash agent-browser screenshot # Screenshot to stdout agent-browser screenshot path.png # Save to file agent-browser screenshot --full # Full page agent-browser pdf output.pdf # Save as PDF ``` ### Video recording ```bash agent-browser record start ./demo.webm # Start recording (uses current URL + state) agent-browser click @e1 # Perform actions agent-browser record stop # Stop and save video agent-browser record restart ./take2.webm # Stop current + start new recording ``` Recording creates a fresh context but preserves cookies/storage from your session. If no URL is provided, it automatically returns to your current page. For smooth demos, explore first, then start recording. ### Wait ```bash agent-browser wait @e1 # Wait for element agent-browser wait 2000 # Wait milliseconds agent-browser wait --text "Success" # Wait for text (or -t) agent-browser wait --url "**/dashboard" # Wait for URL pattern (or -u) agent-browser wait --load networkidle # Wait for network idle (or -l) agent-browser wait --fn "window.ready" # Wait for JS condition (or -f) ``` ### Mouse control ```bash agent-browser mouse move 100 200 # Move mouse agent-browser mouse down left # Press button agent-browser mouse up left # Release button agent-browser mouse wheel 100 # Scroll wheel ``` ### Semantic locators (alternative to refs) ```bash agent-browser find role button click --name "Submit" agent-browser find text "Sign In" click agent-browser find text "Sign In" click --exact # Exact match only agent-browser find label "Email" fill "user@test.com" agent-browser find placeholder "Search" type "query" agent-browser find alt "Logo" click agent-browser find title "Close" click agent-browser find testid "submit-btn" click agent-browser find first ".item" click agent-browser find last ".item" click agent-browser find nth 2 "a" hover ``` ### Browser settings ```bash agent-browser set viewport 1920 1080 # Set viewport size agent-browser set device "iPhone 14" # Emulate device agent-browser set geo 37.7749 -122.4194 # Set geolocation (alias: geolocation) agent-browser set offline on # Toggle offline mode agent-browser set headers '{"X-Key":"v"}' # Extra HTTP headers agent-browser set credentials user pass # HTTP basic auth (alias: auth) agent-browser set media dark # Emulate color scheme agent-browser set media light reduced-motion # Light mode + reduced motion ``` ### Cookies & Storage ```bash agent-browser cookies # Get all cookies agent-browser cookies set name value # Set cookie agent-browser cookies clear # Clear cookies agent-browser storage local # Get all localStorage agent-browser storage local key # Get specific key agent-browser storage local set k v # Set value agent-browser storage local clear # Clear all ``` ### Network ```bash agent-browser network route # Intercept requests agent-browser network route --abort # Block requests agent-browser network route --body '{}' # Mock response agent-browser network unroute [url] # Remove routes agent-browser network requests # View tracked requests agent-browser network requests --filter api # Filter requests ``` ### Tabs & Windows ```bash agent-browser tab # List tabs agent-browser tab new [url] # New tab agent-browser tab 2 # Switch to tab by index agent-browser tab close # Close current tab agent-browser tab close 2 # Close tab by index agent-browser window new # New window ``` ### Frames ```bash agent-browser frame "#iframe" # Switch to iframe agent-browser frame main # Back to main frame ``` ### Dialogs ```bash agent-browser dialog accept [text] # Accept dialog agent-browser dialog dismiss # Dismiss dialog ``` ### JavaScript ```bash agent-browser eval "document.title" # Run JavaScript ``` ## Global options ```bash agent-browser --session ... # Isolated browser session agent-browser --json ... # JSON output for parsing agent-browser --headed ... # Show browser window (not headless) agent-browser --full ... # Full page screenshot (-f) agent-browser --cdp ... # Connect via Chrome DevTools Protocol agent-browser -p ... # Cloud browser provider (--provider) agent-browser --proxy ... # Use proxy server agent-browser --headers ... # HTTP headers scoped to URL's origin agent-browser --executable-path

# Custom browser executable agent-browser --extension ... # Load browser extension (repeatable) agent-browser --help # Show help (-h) agent-browser --version # Show version (-V) agent-browser --help # Show detailed help for a command ``` ### Proxy support ```bash agent-browser --proxy http://proxy.com:8080 open example.com agent-browser --proxy http://user:pass@proxy.com:8080 open example.com agent-browser --proxy socks5://proxy.com:1080 open example.com ``` ## Environment variables ```bash AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION="mysession" # Default session name AGENT_BROWSER_EXECUTABLE_PATH="/path/chrome" # Custom browser path AGENT_BROWSER_EXTENSIONS="/ext1,/ext2" # Comma-separated extension paths AGENT_BROWSER_PROVIDER="browserbase" # Cloud browser provider AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_PORT="9223" # WebSocket streaming port AGENT_BROWSER_HOME="/path/to/agent-browser" # Custom install location (for daemon.js) ``` ## Example: Form submission ```bash agent-browser open https://example.com/form agent-browser snapshot -i # Output shows: textbox "Email" [ref=e1], textbox "Password" [ref=e2], button "Submit" [ref=e3] agent-browser fill @e1 "user@example.com" agent-browser fill @e2 "password123" agent-browser click @e3 agent-browser wait --load networkidle agent-browser snapshot -i # Check result ``` ## Example: Authentication with saved state ```bash # Login once agent-browser open https://app.example.com/login agent-browser snapshot -i agent-browser fill @e1 "username" agent-browser fill @e2 "password" agent-browser click @e3 agent-browser wait --url "**/dashboard" agent-browser state save auth.json # Later sessions: load saved state agent-browser state load auth.json agent-browser open https://app.example.com/dashboard ``` ## Sessions (parallel browsers) ```bash agent-browser --session test1 open site-a.com agent-browser --session test2 open site-b.com agent-browser session list ``` ## JSON output (for parsing) Add `--json` for machine-readable output: ```bash agent-browser snapshot -i --json agent-browser get text @e1 --json ``` ## Debugging ```bash agent-browser --headed open example.com # Show browser window agent-browser --cdp 9222 snapshot # Connect via CDP port agent-browser connect 9222 # Alternative: connect command agent-browser console # View console messages agent-browser console --clear # Clear console agent-browser errors # View page errors agent-browser errors --clear # Clear errors agent-browser highlight @e1 # Highlight element agent-browser trace start # Start recording trace agent-browser trace stop trace.zip # Stop and save trace agent-browser record start ./debug.webm # Record video from current page agent-browser record stop # Save recording ``` ## Deep-dive documentation For detailed patterns and best practices, see: | Reference | Description | | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | | [references/snapshot-refs.md](references/snapshot-refs.md) | Ref lifecycle, invalidation rules, troubleshooting | | [references/session-management.md](references/session-management.md) | Parallel sessions, state persistence, concurrent scraping | | [references/authentication.md](references/authentication.md) | Login flows, OAuth, 2FA handling, state reuse | | [references/video-recording.md](references/video-recording.md) | Recording workflows for debugging and documentation | | [references/proxy-support.md](references/proxy-support.md) | Proxy configuration, geo-testing, rotating proxies | ## Ready-to-use templates Executable workflow scripts for common patterns: | Template | Description | | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | | [templates/form-automation.sh](templates/form-automation.sh) | Form filling with validation | | [templates/authenticated-session.sh](templates/authenticated-session.sh) | Login once, reuse state | | [templates/capture-workflow.sh](templates/capture-workflow.sh) | Content extraction with screenshots | | [templates/launch-chrome-standalone.cjs](templates/launch-chrome-standalone.cjs) | Launch Chrome standalone with debugging support | Usage: ```bash ./templates/form-automation.sh https://example.com/form ./templates/authenticated-session.sh https://app.example.com/login ./templates/capture-workflow.sh https://example.com ./output ``` ## HTTPS Certificate Errors For sites with self-signed or invalid certificates: ```bash agent-browser open https://localhost:8443 --ignore-https-errors ``` Base directory for this skill: file:///D:/Adu/col/js/project/adival/.opencode/skills/agent-browser Relative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory. Note: file list is sampled.       - link "the Web" [ref=e123]:
        - /url: /wiki/World_Wide_Web
      - text: ", alongside"
      - link "HTML" [ref=e124]:
        - /url: /wiki/HTML
      - text: and
      - link "CSS" [ref=e125]:
        - /url: /wiki/CSS
      - text: . It was created by
      - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e126] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
      - text: in 1995.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e127] [nth=2]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - text: As of 2025, the overwhelming majority of
      - link "websites" [ref=e128]:
        - /url: /wiki/Website
      - text: (98.9%) uses JavaScript for
      - link "client" [ref=e129]:
        - /url: /wiki/Client_(computing)
      - text: side
      - link "webpage" [ref=e130]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_page
      - text: behavior.
      - superscript:
        - link "[10]" [ref=e131]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-deployedstats-11"
    - paragraph:
      - link "Web browsers" [ref=e132]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
      - text: have a dedicated
      - link "JavaScript engine" [ref=e133]:
        - /url: /wiki/JavaScript_engine
      - text: that executes the client
      - link "code" [ref=e134]:
        - /url: /wiki/Source_code
      - text: . These engines are also utilized in some
      - link "servers" [ref=e135]:
        - /url: /wiki/Server_(computing)
      - text: and a variety of
      - link "apps" [ref=e136]:
        - /url: /wiki/Application_software
      - text: . The most popular
      - link "runtime system" [ref=e137]:
        - /url: /wiki/Runtime_system
      - text: for non-browser usage is
      - link "Node.js" [ref=e138]:
        - /url: /wiki/Node.js
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[11]" [ref=e139]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-12"
    - paragraph:
      - text: JavaScript is a
      - link "high-level" [ref=e140]:
        - /url: /wiki/High-level_programming_language
      - text: ", often"
      - link "just-in-time–compiled" [ref=e141]:
        - /url: /wiki/Just-in-time_compilation
      - text: language that conforms to the
      - link "ECMAScript" [ref=e142] [nth=3]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript
      - text: standard.
      - superscript:
        - link "[12]" [ref=e143]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-tc39-13"
      - text: It has
      - link "dynamic typing" [ref=e144]:
        - /url: /wiki/Dynamic_typing
      - text: ","
      - link "prototype-based" [ref=e145]:
        - /url: /wiki/Prototype-based_programming
      - link "object-orientation" [ref=e146]:
        - /url: /wiki/Object-oriented_programming
      - text: ", and"
      - link "first-class functions" [ref=e147]:
        - /url: /wiki/First-class_function
      - text: . It is
      - link "multi-paradigm" [ref=e148]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_paradigm
      - text: ", supporting"
      - link "event-driven" [ref=e149] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Event-driven_programming
      - text: ","
      - link "functional" [ref=e150] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Functional_programming
      - text: ", and"
      - link "imperative" [ref=e151] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Imperative_programming
      - link "programming styles" [ref=e152]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_paradigm
      - text: . It has
      - link "application programming interfaces" [ref=e153]:
        - /url: /wiki/Application_programming_interface
      - text: (APIs) for working with text, dates,
      - link "regular expressions" [ref=e154]:
        - /url: /wiki/Regular_expression
      - text: ", standard"
      - link "data structures" [ref=e155]:
        - /url: /wiki/Data_structure
      - text: ", and the"
      - link "Document Object Model" [ref=e156]:
        - /url: /wiki/Document_Object_Model
      - text: (DOM).
    - paragraph:
      - text: The ECMAScript standard does not include any
      - link "input/output" [ref=e157]:
        - /url: /wiki/Input/output
      - text: (I/O), such as
      - link "networking" [ref=e158]:
        - /url: /wiki/Computer_network
      - text: ","
      - link "storage" [ref=e159]:
        - /url: /wiki/Data_storage
      - text: ", or"
      - link "graphics" [ref=e160]:
        - /url: /wiki/Computer_graphics
      - text: facilities. In practice, the web browser or other runtime system provides JavaScript APIs for I/O.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Although
      - link "Java" [ref=e161] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Java_(programming_language)
      - text: and JavaScript are similar in name and
      - link "syntax" [ref=e162]:
        - /url: /wiki/Syntax_(programming_languages)
      - text: ", the two languages are distinct and differ greatly in design."
    - heading "History" [ref=e163] [level=2]
    - heading "Creation at Netscape" [ref=e164] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: The first popular
      - link "web browser" [ref=e165]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
      - text: with a
      - link "graphical user interface" [ref=e166]:
        - /url: /wiki/Graphical_user_interface
      - text: ","
      - link "Mosaic" [ref=e167]:
        - /url: /wiki/Mosaic_(web_browser)
      - text: ", was released in 1993. The lead developers of Mosaic then founded the"
      - link "Netscape" [ref=e168] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape
      - text: corporation, which released a more polished browser,
      - link "Netscape Navigator" [ref=e169]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape_Navigator
      - text: ", in 1994. This quickly became the most-used."
      - superscript:
        - link "[13]" [ref=e170]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-14"
    - paragraph:
      - text: During these formative years of the Web,
      - link "web pages" [ref=e171]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_page
      - text: could only be static, lacking the capability for dynamic behavior after the page was loaded in the browser. There was a desire in the flourishing web development scene to remove this limitation, so in 1995, Netscape decided to add a
      - link "programming language" [ref=e172] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_language
      - text: "to Navigator. They pursued two routes to achieve this: collaborating with"
      - link "Sun Microsystems" [ref=e173]:
        - /url: /wiki/Sun_Microsystems
      - text: to embed the
      - link "Java" [ref=e174] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Java_(programming_language)
      - text: language, while also hiring
      - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e175] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
      - text: to embed the
      - link "Scheme" [ref=e176] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Scheme_(programming_language)
      - text: language.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e177] [nth=3]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The goal was a "language for the masses",
      - superscript:
        - link "[14]" [ref=e178]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
      - text: "\"to help nonprogrammers create dynamic, interactive"
      - link "Web sites" [ref=e179]:
        - /url: /wiki/Website
      - text: "\"."
      - superscript:
        - link "[15]" [ref=e180]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-16"
      - text: Netscape management soon decided that the best option was for Eich to devise a new language, with syntax similar to Java and less like Scheme or other extant
      - link "scripting languages" [ref=e181]:
        - /url: /wiki/Scripting_language
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[5]" [ref=e182] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-looklikejava-5"
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e183] [nth=4]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - text: Although the new language and its
      - link "interpreter" [ref=e184]:
        - /url: /wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
      - text: implementation were called LiveScript when first shipped as part of a Navigator
      - link "beta" [ref=e185]:
        - /url: /wiki/Beta_software
      - text: in September 1995, the name was changed to JavaScript for the official release in December.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e186] [nth=5]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - superscript:
        - link "[1]" [ref=e187] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-press_release-1"
      - superscript:
        - link "[16]" [ref=e188]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-techvision-17"
      - superscript:
        - link "[17]" [ref=e189]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-wired2024-18"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The choice of the JavaScript name has caused confusion, implying that it is directly related to Java. At the time, the
      - link "dot-com boom" [ref=e190]:
        - /url: /wiki/Dot-com_bubble
      - text: had begun and Java was a popular new language, so Eich considered the JavaScript name a marketing ploy by Netscape.
      - superscript:
        - link "[14]" [ref=e191] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
    - heading "Adoption by Microsoft" [ref=e192] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - link "Microsoft" [ref=e193]:
        - /url: /wiki/Microsoft
      - text: debuted
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e194]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: in 1995, leading to a
      - link "browser war" [ref=e195]:
        - /url: /wiki/Browser_war
      - text: with Netscape. On the JavaScript front, Microsoft created its own
      - link "interpreter" [ref=e196] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
      - text: called
      - link "JScript" [ref=e197]:
        - /url: /wiki/JScript
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[18]" [ref=e198]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-sjsch5-19"
    - paragraph:
      - text: Microsoft first released JScript in 1996, alongside initial support for
      - link "CSS" [ref=e199] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/CSS
      - text: and extensions to
      - link "HTML" [ref=e200] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/HTML
      - text: . Each of these
      - link "implementations" [ref=e201] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Implementation
      - text: was noticeably different from their counterparts in
      - link "Netscape Navigator" [ref=e202] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape_Navigator
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[19]" [ref=e203]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-O'Reilly-2001-20"
      - superscript:
        - link "[20]" [ref=e204]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-21"
      - text: These differences made it difficult for developers to make their websites work well in both browsers, leading to widespread use of "best viewed in Netscape" and "best viewed in Internet Explorer" logos for several years.
      - superscript:
        - link "[19]" [ref=e205] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-O'Reilly-2001-20"
      - superscript:
        - link "[21]" [ref=e206]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-22"
    - heading "The rise of JScript" [ref=e207] [level=3]
    - blockquote:
      - paragraph:
        - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e208] [nth=3]:
          - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
        - text: "later said of this period: \"It's still kind of a"
        - link "sidekick" [ref=e209]:
          - /url: /wiki/Sidekick
        - text: language. It's considered slow or annoying. People do
        - link "pop-ups" [ref=e210]:
          - /url: /wiki/Pop-up_ad
        - text: or those scrolling messages in the old
        - link "status bar" [ref=e211]:
          - /url: /wiki/Status_bar
        - text: at the bottom of your old
        - link "browser" [ref=e212]:
          - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
        - text: ."
        - superscript:
          - link "[14]" [ref=e213] [nth=2]:
            - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
    - paragraph:
      - text: In November 1996,
      - link "Netscape" [ref=e214] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape
      - text: submitted JavaScript to
      - link "Ecma International" [ref=e215]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ecma_International
      - text: ", as the starting point for a standard specification that all browser vendors could conform to. This led to the official release of the first"
      - link "ECMAScript" [ref=e216] [nth=4]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript
      - text: language specification in June 1997.
    - paragraph:
      - text: The standards process continued for a few years, with the release of ECMAScript 2 in June 1998 and ECMAScript 3 in December 1999. Work on ECMAScript 4 began in 2000.
      - superscript:
        - link "[18]" [ref=e217] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-sjsch5-19"
    - paragraph:
      - text: However, the effort to fully standardize the language was undermined by Microsoft gaining an increasingly dominant position in the browser market. By the early 2000s,
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e218] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: "'s market share reached 95%."
      - superscript:
        - link "[22]" [ref=e219]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-searchenginejournal.com-23"
      - text: This meant that
      - link "JScript" [ref=e220] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/JScript
      - text: became the de facto standard for
      - link "client-side scripting" [ref=e221]:
        - /url: /wiki/Client-side_scripting
      - text: on the Web.
    - paragraph: Microsoft initially participated in the standards process and implemented some proposals in its JScript language, but eventually it stopped collaborating on ECMA work. Thus ECMAScript 4 was mothballed.
    - heading "Growth and standardization" [ref=e222] [level=3]
    - link "Logo of Ecma International Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard" [ref=e223]:
      - /url: /wiki/File:Logo_of_TC39.svg
      - img "Logo of Ecma International Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard"
    - text: Logo of
    - link "Ecma International" [ref=e224] [nth=1]:
      - /url: /wiki/Ecma_International
    - text: Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard
    - paragraph:
      - text: During the period of
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e225] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: dominance in the early 2000s, client-side scripting was stagnant. This started to change in 2004, when the successor of Netscape,
      - link "Mozilla" [ref=e226]:
        - /url: /wiki/Mozilla
      - text: ", released the"
      - link "Firefox" [ref=e227]:
        - /url: /wiki/Firefox
      - text: browser. Firefox was well received by many, taking significant market share from Internet Explorer.
      - superscript:
        - link "[23]" [ref=e228]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-24"
    - paragraph:
      - text: In 2005, Mozilla joined ECMA International, and work started on the
      - link "ECMAScript for XML" [ref=e229]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript_for_XML
      - text: (E4X) standard. This led to Mozilla working jointly with
      - link "Macromedia" [ref=e230]:
        - /url: /wiki/Macromedia
      - text: (later acquired by
      - link "Adobe Systems" [ref=e231]:
        - /url: /wiki/Adobe_Systems
      - text: ), who were implementing E4X in their ActionScript 3 language, which was based on an ECMAScript 4 draft. The goal became standardizing ActionScript 3 as the new ECMAScript 4. To this end, Adobe Systems released the
      - link "Tamarin" [ref=e232]:
        - /url: /wiki/Tamarin_(software)
      - text: implementation as an
      - link "open source" [ref=e233]:
        - /url: /wiki/Open-source_model
      - text: project. However, Tamarin and ActionScript 3 were too different from established client-side scripting, and without cooperation from Microsoft, ECMAScript 4 never reached fruition.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Meanwhile, very important developments were occurring in open-source communities not affiliated with ECMA work. In 2005,
      - link "Jesse James Garrett" [ref=e234]:
        - /url: /wiki/Jesse_James_Garrett
      - text: released a white paper in which he coined the term
      - link "Ajax" [ref=e235]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ajax_(programming)
      - text: and described a set of technologies, of which JavaScript was the backbone, to create
      - link "web applications" [ref=e236]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_application
      - text: where data can be loaded in the background, avoiding the need for full page reloads. This sparked a renaissance period of JavaScript, spearheaded by open-source libraries and the communities that formed around them. Many new libraries were created, including
      - link "jQuery" [ref=e237]:
        - /url: /wiki/JQuery
      - text: ","
      - link "Prototype" [ref=e238]:
        - /url: /wiki/Prototype_JavaScript_Framework
      - text: ","
      - link "Dojo Toolkit" [ref=e239]:
        - /url: /wiki/Dojo_Toolkit
      - text: ", and"
      - link "MooTools" [ref=e240]:
        - /url: /wiki/MooTools
      - text: .
    - paragraph:
      - link "Google" [ref=e241]:
        - /url: /wiki/Google
      - text: debuted its
      - link "Chrome" [ref=e242]:
        - /url: /wiki/Google_Chrome
      - text: browser in 2008, with the
      - link "V8" [ref=e243] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/V8_(JavaScript_engine)
      - text: JavaScript engine that was faster than its competition.
      - superscript:
        - link "[24]" [ref=e244]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-25"
      - superscript:
        - link "[25]" [ref=e245]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-26"
      - text: The key innovation was
      - link "just-in-time compilation" [ref=e246]:
        - /url: /wiki/Just-in-time_compilation
      - text: (JIT),
      - superscript:
        - link "[26]" [ref=e247]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-27"
      - text: so other browser vendors needed to overhaul their engines for JIT.
      - superscript:
        - link "[27]" [ref=e248]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-28"
    - paragraph:
      - text: In July 2008, these disparate parties came together for a conference in
      - link "Oslo" [ref=e249]:
        - /url: /wiki/Oslo
      - text: . This led to the eventual agreement in early 2009 to combine all relevant work and drive the language forward. The result was the ECMAScript 5 standard, released in December 2009.
    - heading "Reaching maturity" [ref=e250] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Ambitious work on the language continued for several years, culminating in an extensive collection of additions and refinements being formalized with the publication of
      - link "ECMAScript 6" [ref=e251]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript_6
      - text: in 2015.
      - superscript:
        - link "[28]" [ref=e252]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-29"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The creation of
      - link "Node.js" [ref=e253] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Node.js
      - text: in 2009 by
      - link "Ryan Dahl" [ref=e254]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ryan_Dahl
      - text: sparked a significant increase in the usage of JavaScript outside of web browsers. Node combines the
      - link "V8" [ref=e255] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/V8_(JavaScript_engine)
      - text: engine, an
      - link "event loop" [ref=e256]:
        - /url: /wiki/Event_loop
      - text: ", and"
      - link "I/O" [ref=e257]:
        - /url: /wiki/Input/output
      - link "APIs" [ref=e258]:
        - /url: /wiki/Application_programming_interface
      - text: ", thereby providing a stand-alone JavaScript runtime system."
      - superscript:
        - link "[29]" [ref=e259]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-30"
      - superscript:
        - link "[30]" [ref=e260]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-b3-31"
      - text: As of 2018, Node had been used by millions of developers,
      - superscript:
        - link "[31]" [ref=e261]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-32"
      - text: and
      - link "npm" [ref=e262]:
        - /url: /wiki/Npm_(software)
      - text: had the most modules of any
      - link "package manager" [ref=e263]:
        - /url: /wiki/Package_manager
      - text: in the world.
      - superscript:
        - link "[32]" [ref=e264]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-33"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The ECMAScript draft specification is currently maintained openly on
      - link "GitHub" [ref=e265]:
        - /url: /wiki/GitHub
      - text: ","
      - superscript:
        - link "[33]" [ref=e266]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-branscombe-34"
      - text: and editions are produced via regular annual snapshots.
      - superscript:
        - link "[33]" [ref=e267] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-branscombe-34"
      - text: Potential revisions to the language are vetted through a comprehensive proposal process.
      - superscript:
        - link "[34]" [ref=e268]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-35"
      - superscript:
        - link "[35]" [ref=e269]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-36"
      - text: Now, instead of edition numbers, developers check the status of upcoming features individually.
      - superscript:
        - link "[33]" [ref=e270] [nth=2]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-branscombe-34"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The current JavaScript ecosystem has many
      - link "libraries" [ref=e271]:
        - /url: /wiki/List_of_JavaScript_libraries
      - text: and
      - link "frameworks" [ref=e272]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_framework
      - text: ", established programming practices, and substantial usage of JavaScript outside of web browsers."
      - superscript:
        - link "[17]" [ref=e273] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-wired2024-18"
      - text: Plus, with the rise of
      - link "single-page applications" [ref=e274]:
        - /url: /wiki/Single-page_application
      - text: and other JavaScript-heavy websites, several
      - link "transpilers" [ref=e275]:
        - /url: /wiki/Source-to-source_compiler
      - text: have been created to aid the development process.
      - superscript:
        - link "[36]" [ref=e276]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-transpilers-37"
    - heading "Trademark" [ref=e277] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: "\"JavaScript\" is a"
      - link "trademark" [ref=e278]:
        - /url: /wiki/Trademark
      - text: of
      - link "Oracle Corporation" [ref=e279]:
        - /url: /wiki/Oracle_Corporation
      - text: in the United States.
      - superscript:
        - link "[37]" [ref=e280]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-38"
      - superscript:
        - link "[38]" [ref=e281]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-39"
      - text: The trademark was originally issued to
      - link "Sun Microsystems" [ref=e282] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Sun_Microsystems
      - text: on 6 May 1997, and was transferred to Oracle when they acquired Sun in 2009.
      - superscript:
        - link "[39]" [ref=e283]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-40"
      - superscript:
        - link "[40]" [ref=e284]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-41"
    - paragraph:
      - text: A letter was circulated in September 2024, spearheaded by
      - link "Ryan Dahl" [ref=e285] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ryan_Dahl
      - text: ", calling on Oracle to free the JavaScript trademark."
      - superscript:
        - link "[41]" [ref=e286]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-42"
      - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e287] [nth=4]:
        - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
      - text: ", the original creator of JavaScript, was among the over 14,000 signatories who supported the initiative."
    - heading "Website client-side usage" [ref=e288] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: JavaScript is the dominant
      - link "client-side" [ref=e289]:
        - /url: /wiki/Client-side
      - link "scripting language" [ref=e290]:
        - /url: /wiki/Scripting_language
      - text: of the Web, with 99% of all
      - link "websites" [ref=e291] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Website
      - text: using it for this purpose.
      - superscript:
        - link "[10]" [ref=e292] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-deployedstats-11"
      - text: Scripts are embedded in or included from
      - link "HTML" [ref=e293] [nth=2]:

            - text: ","

            - link "AWK" [ref=e103]:

            - link "HyperTalk" [ref=e105]:
              - /url: /wiki/HyperTalk
            - superscript:
              - link "[9]" [ref=e106]:
                - /url: "#cite_note-9"
        - row "Influenced":
          - columnheader "Influenced" [ref=e107]
        - row "ActionScript, ArkTS, AssemblyScript, CoffeeScript, Dart, Haxe, JS++, Opa, TypeScript":
          - cell "ActionScript, ArkTS, AssemblyScript, CoffeeScript, Dart, Haxe, JS++, Opa, TypeScript" [ref=e108]:
            - link "ActionScript" [ref=e109]:
              - /url: /wiki/ActionScript
            - text: ","
            - link "ArkTS" [ref=e110]:

            - text: ","

            - text: ","

            - text: ","

              - /url: /wiki/Dart_(programming_language)
            - text: ","

            - text: ","

              - /url: /wiki/TypeScript
        - row "JavaScript at Wikibooks":
          - cell "JavaScript at Wikibooks" [ref=e118]:
            - list:
              - listitem:
                - link [ref=e119] [nth=1]:
                  - /url: /wiki/File:Wikibooks-logo-en-noslogan.svg
                - link "JavaScript" [ref=e120]:
                  - /url: https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/JavaScript
                - text: at Wikibooks
    - paragraph:
      - text: JavaScript (JS)
      - superscript:
        - link "[a]" [ref=e121]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-10"
      - text: is a
      - link "programming language" [ref=e122]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_language
      - text: and core technology of
      - link "the Web" [ref=e123]:
        - /url: /wiki/World_Wide_Web
      - text: ", alongside"
      - link "HTML" [ref=e124]:
        - /url: /wiki/HTML
      - text: and
      - link "CSS" [ref=e125]:
        - /url: /wiki/CSS
      - text: . It was created by
      - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e126] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
      - text: in 1995.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e127] [nth=2]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - text: As of 2025, the overwhelming majority of
      - link "websites" [ref=e128]:
        - /url: /wiki/Website
      - text: (98.9%) uses JavaScript for
      - link "client" [ref=e129]:
        - /url: /wiki/Client_(computing)
      - text: side
      - link "webpage" [ref=e130]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_page
      - text: behavior.
      - superscript:
        - link "[10]" [ref=e131]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-deployedstats-11"
    - paragraph:
      - link "Web browsers" [ref=e132]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
      - text: have a dedicated
      - link "JavaScript engine" [ref=e133]:
        - /url: /wiki/JavaScript_engine
      - text: that executes the client
      - link "code" [ref=e134]:
        - /url: /wiki/Source_code
      - text: . These engines are also utilized in some
      - link "servers" [ref=e135]:
        - /url: /wiki/Server_(computing)
      - text: and a variety of
      - link "apps" [ref=e136]:
        - /url: /wiki/Application_software
      - text: . The most popular
      - link "runtime system" [ref=e137]:
        - /url: /wiki/Runtime_system
      - text: for non-browser usage is
      - link "Node.js" [ref=e138]:
        - /url: /wiki/Node.js
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[11]" [ref=e139]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-12"
    - paragraph:
      - text: JavaScript is a
      - link "high-level" [ref=e140]:
        - /url: /wiki/High-level_programming_language
      - text: ", often"
      - link "just-in-time–compiled" [ref=e141]:
        - /url: /wiki/Just-in-time_compilation
      - text: language that conforms to the
      - link "ECMAScript" [ref=e142] [nth=3]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript
      - text: standard.
      - superscript:
        - link "[12]" [ref=e143]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-tc39-13"
      - text: It has
      - link "dynamic typing" [ref=e144]:
        - /url: /wiki/Dynamic_typing
      - text: ","
      - link "prototype-based" [ref=e145]:
        - /url: /wiki/Prototype-based_programming
      - link "object-orientation" [ref=e146]:
        - /url: /wiki/Object-oriented_programming
      - text: ", and"
      - link "first-class functions" [ref=e147]:
        - /url: /wiki/First-class_function
      - text: . It is
      - link "multi-paradigm" [ref=e148]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_paradigm
      - text: ", supporting"
      - link "event-driven" [ref=e149] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Event-driven_programming
      - text: ","
      - link "functional" [ref=e150] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Functional_programming
      - text: ", and"
      - link "imperative" [ref=e151] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Imperative_programming
      - link "programming styles" [ref=e152]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_paradigm
      - text: . It has
      - link "application programming interfaces" [ref=e153]:
        - /url: /wiki/Application_programming_interface
      - text: (APIs) for working with text, dates,
      - link "regular expressions" [ref=e154]:
        - /url: /wiki/Regular_expression
      - text: ", standard"
      - link "data structures" [ref=e155]:
        - /url: /wiki/Data_structure
      - text: ", and the"
      - link "Document Object Model" [ref=e156]:
        - /url: /wiki/Document_Object_Model
      - text: (DOM).
    - paragraph:
      - text: The ECMAScript standard does not include any
      - link "input/output" [ref=e157]:
        - /url: /wiki/Input/output
      - text: (I/O), such as
      - link "networking" [ref=e158]:
        - /url: /wiki/Computer_network
      - text: ","
      - link "storage" [ref=e159]:
        - /url: /wiki/Data_storage
      - text: ", or"
      - link "graphics" [ref=e160]:
        - /url: /wiki/Computer_graphics
      - text: facilities. In practice, the web browser or other runtime system provides JavaScript APIs for I/O.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Although
      - link "Java" [ref=e161] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Java_(programming_language)
      - text: and JavaScript are similar in name and
      - link "syntax" [ref=e162]:
        - /url: /wiki/Syntax_(programming_languages)
      - text: ", the two languages are distinct and differ greatly in design."
    - heading "History" [ref=e163] [level=2]
    - heading "Creation at Netscape" [ref=e164] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: The first popular
      - link "web browser" [ref=e165]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
      - text: with a
      - link "graphical user interface" [ref=e166]:
        - /url: /wiki/Graphical_user_interface
      - text: ","
      - link "Mosaic" [ref=e167]:
        - /url: /wiki/Mosaic_(web_browser)
      - text: ", was released in 1993. The lead developers of Mosaic then founded the"
      - link "Netscape" [ref=e168] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape
      - text: corporation, which released a more polished browser,
      - link "Netscape Navigator" [ref=e169]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape_Navigator
      - text: ", in 1994. This quickly became the most-used."
      - superscript:
        - link "[13]" [ref=e170]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-14"
    - paragraph:
      - text: During these formative years of the Web,
      - link "web pages" [ref=e171]:
        - /url: /wiki/Web_page
      - text: could only be static, lacking the capability for dynamic behavior after the page was loaded in the browser. There was a desire in the flourishing web development scene to remove this limitation, so in 1995, Netscape decided to add a
      - link "programming language" [ref=e172] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Programming_language
      - text: "to Navigator. They pursued two routes to achieve this: collaborating with"
      - link "Sun Microsystems" [ref=e173]:
        - /url: /wiki/Sun_Microsystems
      - text: to embed the
      - link "Java" [ref=e174] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Java_(programming_language)
      - text: language, while also hiring
      - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e175] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
      - text: to embed the
      - link "Scheme" [ref=e176] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Scheme_(programming_language)
      - text: language.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e177] [nth=3]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The goal was a "language for the masses",
      - superscript:
        - link "[14]" [ref=e178]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
      - text: "\"to help nonprogrammers create dynamic, interactive"
      - link "Web sites" [ref=e179]:
        - /url: /wiki/Website
      - text: "\"."
      - superscript:
        - link "[15]" [ref=e180]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-16"
      - text: Netscape management soon decided that the best option was for Eich to devise a new language, with syntax similar to Java and less like Scheme or other extant
      - link "scripting languages" [ref=e181]:
        - /url: /wiki/Scripting_language
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[5]" [ref=e182] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-looklikejava-5"
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e183] [nth=4]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - text: Although the new language and its
      - link "interpreter" [ref=e184]:
        - /url: /wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
      - text: implementation were called LiveScript when first shipped as part of a Navigator
      - link "beta" [ref=e185]:
        - /url: /wiki/Beta_software
      - text: in September 1995, the name was changed to JavaScript for the official release in December.
      - superscript:
        - link "[6]" [ref=e186] [nth=5]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-origin-6"
      - superscript:
        - link "[1]" [ref=e187] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-press_release-1"
      - superscript:
        - link "[16]" [ref=e188]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-techvision-17"
      - superscript:
        - link "[17]" [ref=e189]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-wired2024-18"
    - paragraph:
      - text: The choice of the JavaScript name has caused confusion, implying that it is directly related to Java. At the time, the
      - link "dot-com boom" [ref=e190]:
        - /url: /wiki/Dot-com_bubble
      - text: had begun and Java was a popular new language, so Eich considered the JavaScript name a marketing ploy by Netscape.
      - superscript:
        - link "[14]" [ref=e191] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
    - heading "Adoption by Microsoft" [ref=e192] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - link "Microsoft" [ref=e193]:
        - /url: /wiki/Microsoft
      - text: debuted
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e194]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: in 1995, leading to a
      - link "browser war" [ref=e195]:
        - /url: /wiki/Browser_war
      - text: with Netscape. On the JavaScript front, Microsoft created its own
      - link "interpreter" [ref=e196] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Interpreter_(computing)
      - text: called
      - link "JScript" [ref=e197]:
        - /url: /wiki/JScript
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[18]" [ref=e198]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-sjsch5-19"
    - paragraph:
      - text: Microsoft first released JScript in 1996, alongside initial support for
      - link "CSS" [ref=e199] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/CSS
      - text: and extensions to
      - link "HTML" [ref=e200] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/HTML
      - text: . Each of these
      - link "implementations" [ref=e201] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Implementation
      - text: was noticeably different from their counterparts in
      - link "Netscape Navigator" [ref=e202] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape_Navigator
      - text: .
      - superscript:
        - link "[19]" [ref=e203]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-O'Reilly-2001-20"
      - superscript:
        - link "[20]" [ref=e204]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-21"
      - text: These differences made it difficult for developers to make their websites work well in both browsers, leading to widespread use of "best viewed in Netscape" and "best viewed in Internet Explorer" logos for several years.
      - superscript:
        - link "[19]" [ref=e205] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-O'Reilly-2001-20"
      - superscript:
        - link "[21]" [ref=e206]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-22"
    - heading "The rise of JScript" [ref=e207] [level=3]
    - blockquote:
      - paragraph:
        - link "Brendan Eich" [ref=e208] [nth=3]:
          - /url: /wiki/Brendan_Eich
        - text: "later said of this period: \"It's still kind of a"
        - link "sidekick" [ref=e209]:
          - /url: /wiki/Sidekick
        - text: language. It's considered slow or annoying. People do
        - link "pop-ups" [ref=e210]:
          - /url: /wiki/Pop-up_ad
        - text: or those scrolling messages in the old
        - link "status bar" [ref=e211]:
          - /url: /wiki/Status_bar
        - text: at the bottom of your old
        - link "browser" [ref=e212]:
          - /url: /wiki/Web_browser
        - text: ."
        - superscript:
          - link "[14]" [ref=e213] [nth=2]:
            - /url: "#cite_note-EichVideo-15"
    - paragraph:
      - text: In November 1996,
      - link "Netscape" [ref=e214] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Netscape
      - text: submitted JavaScript to
      - link "Ecma International" [ref=e215]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ecma_International
      - text: ", as the starting point for a standard specification that all browser vendors could conform to. This led to the official release of the first"
      - link "ECMAScript" [ref=e216] [nth=4]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript
      - text: language specification in June 1997.
    - paragraph:
      - text: The standards process continued for a few years, with the release of ECMAScript 2 in June 1998 and ECMAScript 3 in December 1999. Work on ECMAScript 4 began in 2000.
      - superscript:
        - link "[18]" [ref=e217] [nth=1]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-sjsch5-19"
    - paragraph:
      - text: However, the effort to fully standardize the language was undermined by Microsoft gaining an increasingly dominant position in the browser market. By the early 2000s,
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e218] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: "'s market share reached 95%."
      - superscript:
        - link "[22]" [ref=e219]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-searchenginejournal.com-23"
      - text: This meant that
      - link "JScript" [ref=e220] [nth=1]:
        - /url: /wiki/JScript
      - text: became the de facto standard for
      - link "client-side scripting" [ref=e221]:
        - /url: /wiki/Client-side_scripting
      - text: on the Web.
    - paragraph: Microsoft initially participated in the standards process and implemented some proposals in its JScript language, but eventually it stopped collaborating on ECMA work. Thus ECMAScript 4 was mothballed.
    - heading "Growth and standardization" [ref=e222] [level=3]
    - link "Logo of Ecma International Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard" [ref=e223]:
      - /url: /wiki/File:Logo_of_TC39.svg
      - img "Logo of Ecma International Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard"
    - text: Logo of
    - link "Ecma International" [ref=e224] [nth=1]:
      - /url: /wiki/Ecma_International
    - text: Technical Committee 39 who is responsible for maintaining the standard
    - paragraph:
      - text: During the period of
      - link "Internet Explorer" [ref=e225] [nth=2]:
        - /url: /wiki/Internet_Explorer
      - text: dominance in the early 2000s, client-side scripting was stagnant. This started to change in 2004, when the successor of Netscape,
      - link "Mozilla" [ref=e226]:
        - /url: /wiki/Mozilla
      - text: ", released the"
      - link "Firefox" [ref=e227]:
        - /url: /wiki/Firefox
      - text: browser. Firefox was well received by many, taking significant market share from Internet Explorer.
      - superscript:
        - link "[23]" [ref=e228]:
          - /url: "#cite_note-24"
    - paragraph:
      - text: In 2005, Mozilla joined ECMA International, and work started on the
      - link "ECMAScript for XML" [ref=e229]:
        - /url: /wiki/ECMAScript_for_XML
      - text: (E4X) standard. This led to Mozilla working jointly with
      - link "Macromedia" [ref=e230]:
        - /url: /wiki/Macromedia
      - text: (later acquired by
      - link "Adobe Systems" [ref=e231]:
        - /url: /wiki/Adobe_Systems
      - text: ), who were implementing E4X in their ActionScript 3 language, which was based on an ECMAScript 4 draft. The goal became standardizing ActionScript 3 as the new ECMAScript 4. To this end, Adobe Systems released the
      - link "Tamarin" [ref=e232]:
        - /url: /wiki/Tamarin_(software)
      - text: implementation as an
      - link "open source" [ref=e233]:
        - /url: /wiki/Open-source_model
      - text: project. However, Tamarin and ActionScript 3 were too different from established client-side scripting, and without cooperation from Microsoft, ECMAScript 4 never reached fruition.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Meanwhile, very important developments were occurring in open-source communities not affiliated with ECMA work. In 2005,
      - link "Jesse James Garrett" [ref=e234]:
        - /url: /wiki/Jesse_James_Garrett
      - text: released a white paper in which he coined the term
      - link "Ajax" [ref=e235]:
        - /url: /wiki/Ajax_(programming)
      - text: and described a set of technologies, of which JavaScript was the backbone, to create
      - link "web app

...
